turns.content: I've read about async and futures but I still don't fully understand how it works under the hood. Can you help?
Sure, let's break down why async functions are just wrappers over a concrete trait implementation called Future in Rust.
I thought every async function automatically became a task that runs on its own thread?
That's a common misconception. In Rust, async functions do not run on separate threads; they produce Future objects that can be polled by the runtime or executor.
So when I write an async function it just returns something?
Exactly, an async fn returns a Future that implements the Future trait. The actual work happens via a poll method on this trait.
But I thought there was some kind of context or waker involved?
Yes, the Future trait requires a poll method and often uses context for scheduling. The waker is used to notify when something needs to be polled again.
I see now that async functions don't just magically run in parallel but produce futures with specific behavior?
Precisely. The key insight is that the runtime polls these Future objects repeatedly until completion.
So if I manually implement a simple Future, I need to define poll and context?
Correct. You must implement the core methods required by the trait like poll and potentially waker handling.
This clarifies how async is just a syntactic sugar over manual Future implementations in Rust?
Yes, that's exactly the connection. The compiler transforms your async fn into an impl of the Future trait.

I've been staring at this async code for days and I still don't get how the compiler actually turns my async fn into something it can run. I read about the Future trait but I'm confused why we need to manually write a Poll method if I just want to use an async function.

The key thing to realize is that when you define an async function, the Rust compiler automatically generates a closure that implements the std::future::Future trait for you. It wraps your logic inside a struct and provides the necessary methods like Poll and Waker so it can work with the executor. You don't write this manually unless you are implementing a custom future or debugging exactly how the desugaring happens.

Okay, but I still don't see why we need to poll the state at all instead of just waiting on some kind of signal like an event loop in other languages. It feels like extra boilerplate if the runtime is supposed to handle the timing anyway.

Think of it like a stopwatch that you have to check every second to see if time has passed. The Poll method is essentially asking the system, Have we been notified? or Is enough time up? If the answer is yes, then we can move forward and return Ok(()). This manual checking allows the executor to suspend our function until an event happens without blocking the thread.

That analogy makes a bit more sense but I'm still struggling with understanding why the context parameter exists in Poll. Why isn't it enough for us to just have access to the waker? What does this extra piece of information actually do that changes how async works compared to simple threading.

The Context trait is like a shared memory space between your future and the executor. While the Waker tells you when to wake up, the Context provides the necessary metadata about what kind of suspension state we are in right now. It allows the executor to store information about how many times you've been polled so it can properly manage the lifecycle of your async function.

I'm getting confused again because I see some code where they use a local variable for context but then pass an argument into Poll. If I just want to keep this simple, why do we need to manually handle these parameters instead of letting Rust manage everything like it does with regular functions?

When you write async fn, the compiler generates the Future implementation but sometimes you might need more control over how that future behaves or optimize specific parts. By manually implementing Poll and Context, you are taking full ownership of how your function interacts with the runtime instead of relying on the generated code. This is useful when you want to implement complex synchronization logic or debug exactly how the executor sees your state.

This feels like a lot of overhead for something that should be handled automatically by the language. Are we really going back and forth between these manual implementations and async functions just because it's more efficient? Can't we just stick to using async/await and trust the compiler to handle this complexity without us needing to understand Poll or Waker at all?

You are absolutely right for most cases. For 99% of applications, you should just use async/await and let the compiler do the heavy lifting with its desugaring mechanism. You only need to manually implement these traits when you are writing high-performance code or debugging edge cases where the standard behavior isn't enough.

Alright so my takeaway is that I can stop worrying about Poll and Waker for normal projects but if I ever do want to understand how async works under the hood, what exactly should I be looking at in the generated code? Is there a specific pattern or trait I should focus on when inspecting the compiler output?

When you look at the compiler output, focus on the generated struct that implements Future. Look for the Poll method implementation and see how it handles the state transitions. You will notice that the compiler creates a closure that captures your variables and then uses the Context trait to manage the lifecycle of your future throughout its execution.
I've been reading the blog post about async functions and futures, but I still don't get how async fn actually works under the hood. It seems like it just desugars into something else.

That's a great question. You're right that async fn is syntactic sugar for implementing the Future trait. When you write an async function, the compiler actually generates code where you implement the core of the Future trait manually inside that function.
So what does that implementation look like exactly? I'm trying to understand the structure of a manual Future implementation.

A manual Future needs three main pieces: a state variable, a Poll method, and an optional Waker. The state tracks whether you're ready or paused. The Poll method is the heart of it—it returns Ready if done, Pending if not yet ready, or InProgress to indicate we're in the middle of running.
I think I'm getting confused about Poll and Context. Is Context just a wrapper around some runtime data?

Not quite. Context is actually the runtime environment that provides the Waker mechanism. The Waker lets you notify when things change externally, like new I/O or timer events. It's not a wrapper per se; it's how the runtime tells your Future when to wake up and resume.
Ah okay, so if I'm writing my own async function, do I need to manually manage all that state?

Yes, you do. The compiler doesn't hide the complexity from you. You still have to track your execution state and implement Poll correctly. That's why understanding the Future trait is so important—it's not just a concept; it's what actually powers async.
Can you explain how Poll interacts with Context? I feel like they're more connected than I thought.

They work together. When Poll returns Pending, the runtime uses the Waker from your context to schedule a wake-up event. The next time that event happens, it calls your Future's Poll again with the updated state. So Context isn't just metadata; it's how you integrate into the async execution flow.
This is getting clearer now. But I'm still wondering why we even have this manual mechanism when async fn seems so convenient.

The convenience comes from the compiler doing the heavy lifting of generating that Future implementation for you. But understanding it manually gives you control over performance and behavior, which is useful in advanced scenarios or when debugging complex async code.
Got it. So if I want to implement a simple Future, what's the minimum structure I need?

You just need a struct with a state field and an impl block for Future. Inside that impl, you define Poll() -> Poll<Output> and optionally store a Waker if you need external notifications. The rest is handled by the standard library's runtime.

I just wrote a function that uses async and it works, but I'm wondering if there's a more idiomatic way to structure this. My current code looks like this: async fn run_task() -> Result<(), Error> { let mut x = 0; loop { match Some(1) { None => break Ok(()), _ => {} } } } Does using an explicit loop here feel right or should I be doing something else with the Future trait?

Your current approach is functional but not idiomatic. The best practice in Rust for this scenario is to use the async keyword which desugars your function into a concrete impl of the Future trait. When you write an async fn, it automatically implements the Future trait for that specific type. This means you don't need to manually implement Poll, Context, or Waker unless you are building something like tokio::time::sleep with custom backpressure logic.
I see, so I just let Rust handle the Future implementation for me? But what if I really wanted to understand how it works under the hood and implement my own version of a simple future manually?

That is a great question. Yes, you can absolutely implement your own Future trait manually using the standard async::Future methods like poll, context, or waker. This gives you full control over when the task should be suspended. For example, you might define struct MyTask { state: bool } and then implement the Future trait for it with a poll method that returns Pending until some condition is met.
So I would have to write out the entire interface myself? That seems tedious compared to just using async fn?

You are correct, manual implementation is more verbose. However, understanding how Poll and Waker work helps you reason about concurrency better. The key difference is that async fn provides a high-level abstraction while manual Future traits give you low-level control over thread scheduling and wake-up mechanisms.
I still think my loop-based approach was fine because it works. Why do people insist on using the Future trait if I can just write loops?

The reason is that async/await syntax is syntactic sugar designed specifically to make working with Future traits easier. It handles the boilerplate of implementing Poll, Context, and Waker for you. But remember that under the hood, every async function still implements Future trait, so your loop-based code is likely just a workaround rather than idiomatic Rust.
Okay, I get it now. So if I want to avoid the boilerplate, I should always use async fn instead of manually implementing traits?

Exactly for most cases. You only need manual Future implementations when you are building libraries or tools that require fine-grained control over execution flow, such as custom timers or sophisticated concurrency primitives.
This makes sense. But wait, I thought async functions always returned Future trait instances? So why do tutorials spend so much time explaining how to implement the trait manually?

Tutorials explain manual implementation because it helps you understand what async fn actually does. The async keyword is just syntactic sugar that tells the compiler to generate code implementing Future for your function type.
Oh, so I was misunderstanding something fundamental about how Rust handles async code? Like maybe thinking async and Future are completely separate things?

You hit the nail on the head. That is exactly the misconception. Async functions do not exist separately from Future; they are simply a convenient way to write implementations of the Future trait without manually writing the boilerplate code.

That clears it up! So my original loop-based approach wasn't wrong, just not what Rust developers typically use for this kind of problem. Is there any case where I should stick with manual Future traits instead?
Yes, if you are building a library that needs to be used by other crates and want to expose the underlying mechanism without forcing users to understand async/await syntax manually.
I'm working on a new networking library and I need to implement a custom future, but my code compiles with errors. Can someone explain what's happening?

It sounds like you might be missing the specific trait implementation required for async execution. The compiler is asking you to explicitly implement the Future trait because that's what defines an object capable of being awaited.
I tried using std::future::Future but it still doesn't work. What exactly does this trait require? I need to know how to structure my code to satisfy its requirements.
The core requirement is a single method called poll that takes a context and a state variable, returns an Option ready to indicate if the future is done or needs more computation.
I see. But what about this Context parameter? It seems like some futures just work without needing me to handle it manually.

That's exactly why you need to implement it yourself - standard async functions desugar into a concrete impl Future that wraps your logic, but when you write raw code you must provide the context handling.
Okay so I need to pass a Context object every time poll is called. Is there something specific about this type that makes it special? And what exactly does the Waker field do if I were to use one?
The Context holds both the Poll state and the Waker which notifies when the future should resume execution.
I want to understand how the Waker actually gets invoked. When exactly is it called during execution? Is there a specific mechanism in the runtime that triggers this notification?
The runtime tracks the waker and invokes it whenever I yield control back to it, allowing the future to resume later.
That makes sense for resuming, but what if my future is just simple logic without any yielding? Do I still need to implement this complex Context structure or can I simplify it?
Even simple futures require the trait implementation because async fn desugars into Future regardless of complexity.
I understand now. But what if I want to avoid implementing poll manually and instead use a simpler approach for my basic networking logic? Are there crates that provide ready-made implementations?
There are crates like tokio::net or async-std that wrap this complexity, but you still need to ensure your code conforms to the trait requirements.
Wait, I think I misunderstood something. If I'm not manually implementing poll with Context and Waker, how does my custom networking logic actually become an awaitable future in the first place?
You're right to question that - you must either use a macro or crate helper to generate the Future implementation, or write it yourself.

So I cannot just call async on a regular function and expect it to work without understanding this trait? It seems like there's always an underlying complexity even when using high-level abstractions.
You're absolutely correct - every awaitable operation eventually reduces to the same Future trait mechanics.
Can you give me a concrete example of how this looks in actual Rust code for my networking library? I want to see exactly what poll and waker look like when I'm handling socket operations.
Sure, here is a basic skeleton showing the trait implementation with the poll method taking context and returning PollOutcome.
I need to understand how the state variable changes between calls. Can you explain exactly what happens in memory when poll returns Ready vs Pending?
When poll returns Ready, the future is considered complete; otherwise it updates the internal state for next iterations.

I got a compile error on my new async task runner. The message says I need to implement the Poll method correctly but I'm getting 'type mismatch' errors everywhere. I pasted the code: fn run() -> impl Future<Item = Result<(), E>> { let mut state = State::new(); loop { match state.poll() { Ok(Ready(v)) => return v, Err(E::Timeout) => break } } }

The issue is that you are trying to return `impl Future` directly from a function in a context where the trait bound isn't satisfied. In modern Rust, async functions desugar into `impl Future`, so your closure needs to explicitly implement `Future<Item = Result<(), E>>`. The compiler expects a concrete type or a specific trait implementation, not an opaque block like that. You need to wrap the loop in an `async fn` or manually box it as `Box<dyn Future<Output = ...>>`.

That makes sense about the desugaring but I'm still confused about why my manual implementation of Poll isn't working. I tried using a static reference to state instead of moving it into the closure: fn poll(&mut self) -> Poll<Ready<Result<(), E>>, NotReady> { ... }

You're mixing up the old `Poll` return type with the new Rust 2018 syntax. The `Poll` enum and associated methods like `poll` are deprecated in favor of `Async` and `Future`. Your function must implement `impl Future<Output = Result<(), E>>`, which means you cannot use `Poll` at all. Instead, your `poll` method should return a `Poll` enum only if you were writing for older versions, but since you're using Rust 2018+, the trait requires returning `Async` values or implementing the new `Future` trait directly.
I see. So I should just use async fn and forget about Poll? But what if I want to optimize a specific edge case where context is needed?

When you manually implement `impl Future<Output = ...>`, the compiler generates a `Context` struct that holds references and state. If your logic requires external resources like threads or channels, you can use the `Waker` trait to notify when those resources are ready. For example, if you're waiting on a channel, you'd store a `ChannelReceiver` in your `State`, then implement `poll` to check if data is available; otherwise, return `Poll::Pending` and set up a `Waker` that wakes the task when the channel sends something.
Wait, I thought async fn just desugared automatically. Why do I need to manually implement Future if I can just use async?

You don't *need* to manually implement `Future` for most cases—async functions are convenient wrappers that generate the implementation for you. However, manual implementation is useful when you need fine-grained control over the task's lifecycle or want to optimize specific edge cases like reducing allocations or managing state transitions explicitly. In your case, since you're building a tool for repetitive tasks, manually implementing `Future` lets you avoid unnecessary overhead from the desugared version.
Okay, so I should just use async fn and let the compiler handle it? But what if my logic depends on context?

If your logic heavily relies on shared state or external resources that need to be managed during polling, manual implementation gives you more control. For example, imagine a scenario where you're processing tasks from a queue and need to track progress manually. With async fn, the compiler generates a generic `Context` but doesn't expose its internals easily. Manual implementation lets you customize how `poll` interacts with your specific data structures.
I'm still stuck on why my Poll method isn't returning Async correctly. Can I use async block syntax inside the Future?

You can use `async` blocks inside your `Future` implementation, but you need to ensure they're wrapped properly. For example, inside your `poll` method, you might return an `Async` value or call a helper function that returns the correct type. The key is making sure every path in your logic either completes with `Ready(Result)` or suspends with `Poll::Pending`. If you're using async blocks within your manual implementation, remember to use `impl Future` syntax correctly.
So the issue was my Poll return type? But I thought async fn just desugars everything automatically?

Yes, exactly. The `async` keyword desugars into `impl Future<Output = ...>`, but if you're manually implementing the trait or using older syntax like `Poll`, you must ensure compatibility with Rust 2018 standards. Your error likely came from trying to mix deprecated `Poll` returns with modern async contexts. The solution is to either use `async fn` for automatic desugaring or implement `impl Future<Output = ...>` explicitly without relying on `Poll`.

I understand now. But what about the Context struct that async fn creates? Do I need to manage it manually?

One more thing—can I reuse this pattern for other async tasks in my tool?

Absolutely. Once you understand the `Future` trait and how to implement it correctly, you can apply the same pattern to multiple async tasks. The key is ensuring your logic returns either `Ready(Result)` or `Poll::Pending` consistently. For repetitive tasks like yours, this manual approach often provides better performance than relying solely on automatic desugaring.
Thanks! I think I get it now. Just to confirm—my Poll method should never return Poll anymore?

Correct. In Rust 2018 and later, `Poll` is deprecated because the new `Future` trait uses a simpler design where you only need to return `Ready(Result)` or `Poll::Pending`. If you're implementing `impl Future`, your logic should align with this modern standard. The compiler will reject any code that tries to use `Poll` in an incompatible way.
Got it! One last question—what if I want to add a timeout mechanism?

For timeouts, you can use the `Waker` trait in your manual implementation. When the timer expires, set up a `Waker` that notifies the runtime to wake your task. Inside your `poll` method, check if the timeout has elapsed; if it hasn't, return `Poll::Pending`. If it has, return `Ready(TimeoutResult)`. This approach integrates seamlessly with async contexts and avoids manual polling of timers.
What's the difference between an async function returning a future and manually implementing the Future trait with Poll, Context, and Waker?

When you write an async fn, it automatically desugars into something that implements the Future trait. The key distinction is that async functions provide high-level convenience while manual implementation gives you direct control over the Poll state machine and the context passed to it. In your book exercises, implementing Future manually means you explicitly define how to handle the current step in the Poll method rather than relying on the compiler's generated logic.
So if I just use async fn, I don't need to worry about the Context or Waker fields at all? That seems like it would be lazy and inefficient compared to writing a full state machine by hand.

That is a common misconception. While async fn does simplify things, it still requires an underlying implementation of Future under the hood which must handle those fields correctly for correctness. The difference is that async fn handles the boilerplate of setting up the context and waking threads for you, but if you need custom logic or specific wake conditions that don't fit standard patterns, manual implementation becomes necessary.
Right, so I could write a simple Poll function manually to track progress through steps without needing the full async machinery?

You can definitely write a minimal Poll function, but you still need to implement Context and Waker. The Context allows you to pass information between steps like the current index or error state, while Waker enables the task to be resumed when I/O completes. Without these, your manual Poll implementation would likely deadlock because there's no mechanism for rescheduling.
Ah okay, but what if my book exercise only asks me to implement a basic Poll and doesn't mention Context or Waker? Should I still include them even though they aren't required by the prompt?

You should definitely include them. Even for simple exercises, omitting Context and Waker can lead to subtle correctness issues that become apparent later when you try to integrate it into a larger system. The book likely expects you to see that these components are foundational to the async model rather than optional extras.
True, but manually implementing Future feels much more verbose than just calling the async function. Is there any real performance benefit to doing it this way?

There is no inherent performance difference between manual implementation and using async fn since they both compile down to the same core logic. The value lies in understanding how the compiler transforms your code. When you manually implement Future, you see exactly what Poll does internally, which helps you optimize complex state machines later.
That makes sense, but if there's no performance gain, why do advanced patterns sometimes use manual implementation instead of async fn?

Advanced patterns often use manual implementation because they need to customize the behavior that isn't exposed through the standard Future interface. For example, you might need specific wake conditions or custom context handling that requires direct access to the Poll method and its parameters.
Okay, so if I'm doing a basic exercise, should I stick with async fn unless explicitly told otherwise?
Yes, for most exercises, start with async fn. Only switch to manual implementation when you need to handle edge cases or understand the underlying mechanics deeply.
Got it, but what exactly does Poll return? Does it tell me whether I'm done or if I should continue?

Poll returns a Result<Output, Poll::Error>. A Ok(value) means you've completed and returned the output. An Err(Poll::Pending) indicates you're not done yet and need to resume later. The specific error variant tells you why you can't proceed.
So if I'm stuck on a step in my manual implementation, how do I know whether it's because of an error or just that I haven't reached completion?

You check the return value of Poll. If it returns Ok(value), you're done and can drop the future. If it returns Err(Poll::Pending) or any other error variant, you need to handle that specific condition before attempting to resume again.
Understood. One last thing, why does the Future trait require a Send + Sync bound on Output? What happens if I don't satisfy this constraint?

The Send + Sync bounds ensure that your future can be safely moved across threads and used in contexts requiring synchronization. If you violate these constraints, the compiler will reject your code because the runtime cannot guarantee thread safety when passing futures between tasks or waiting on them.

I'm back in the game after five years and trying to understand why my async code feels so different now. Imagine I have a simple side project where I need to wait for some external data before continuing, but I'm confused about how the compiler actually handles this under the hood versus what I write.

Welcome back! That's a great starting point. When you write async fn in Rust today, it doesn't just run in parallel; it desugars into an implementation of the Future trait. The compiler automatically creates a state machine that implements Poll and Waker methods for your function, so when you actually need to manually implement this logic, you're essentially building that same state machine yourself.
Right, I see the Future trait now, but what exactly is Poll? It seems like it's just a function that returns an enum result,

Exactly. Poll takes a mutable reference to your context and a Result type—specifically Poll::Pending or Poll::Ready(value). The compiler-generated async functions use this trait, but when you implement Future manually, you're defining how the state advances by calling Poll repeatedly until it returns Ready with your data.
And what about Context? Is that just a wrapper around the runtime state?

Context is more than just a wrapper; it's the actual storage for your Future's internal state. When you write an async fn, the compiler generates a Context struct that holds everything needed to track progress—like whether you're waiting on I/O or have completed execution. If you are implementing your own Future manually, you must define this Context type yourself and manage its fields.
I'm trying to visualize how Poll interacts with Waker. Can you explain the relationship between those two?

Poll is about advancing state, while Waker is about notifying others when that state changes. When your Future completes execution in Poll::Ready, it calls waker.wake() so any waiting task can resume. If you're implementing this manually, you need to ensure your Context contains a reference to the Waker trait and handles the wake-up logic correctly.
Okay, so if I want to implement my own simple Future for side project data fetching, what's the basic structure?

You'd define a struct with fields like state (initialized to Pending) and maybe some data. Then you create an impl block that implements Future<Output=YourDataType> with methods Poll(self: &mut Self) -> Poll::Ready(YourDataType) or Poll::Pending and waker(&mut self). You start in the Pending state, wait for your external event, then return Ready when done.
What happens if I forget to implement waker? My code will just panic?

Not necessarily panic immediately, but you'll get a compilation error because the Future trait requires waker. At runtime, if you try to wake someone without a valid Waker implementation, it could cause deadlocks or unexpected behavior since no one gets notified about completion.
I think I'm starting to understand how async fn becomes impl Future now,

Yes, the compiler essentially generates a struct that implements Future<Output=YourReturnType> with all required methods. This means when you call an async function, it's really calling Poll on this hidden state machine.
Can I optimize my manual Future implementation by reducing how much Context data I store?

You can definitely reduce the size of your Context struct if you only need minimal state tracking. For example, instead of storing full event details in every iteration, you might just keep a simple boolean flag or an index counter to track which step of execution you're on.
What about thread safety? Does my manual Future implementation need to be Send + Sync?
Yes, if your side project needs to run across threads, your Context type must implement Send and Sync. This ensures Poll can safely transfer ownership between threads without data races.
I'm still struggling with the concept of state transitions in my manual Future,

Focus on how each Poll call advances your state machine from one condition to another. Think of it as a simple loop where you check conditions, update state variables, and return either Pending or Ready based on whether your external event has occurred yet.
One last thing—what's the practical benefit of understanding this manual Future vs using async fn?

Manual Futures give you full control over the lifecycle and state management without relying on the compiler's generated code. This is useful when you need custom behavior, like tracking multiple events or implementing specific synchronization patterns that standard async functions can't handle directly.

I'm trying to port our old blocking codebase to async Rust, but I keep hitting a wall when the compiler tells me I need to implement Poll and Context. It feels like I'm just copying boilerplate without understanding what's actually happening under the hood.

The confusion comes from thinking that async functions are magic black boxes. In reality, every `async fn` is desugared by the compiler into a function returning an `impl Future`. When you call it, you get back a concrete type like my custom `MyTaskFuture`, which must then implement the core trait requirements: Poll and Waker.

Okay, so I need to manually write these traits? That sounds tedious compared to just using `tokio::spawn` or `async-std`. Why would anyone bother with this manual implementation when the ecosystem provides macros and libraries that handle all of this for us?

You are absolutely right about the convenience, but understanding the manual implementation is crucial because it reveals how the runtime actually cooperates with your code. The trait requires three things: a `poll` method to check if work is done, a `context` field that holds shared state like a thread-local context or a guard for synchronization primitives, and a `waker` mechanism to notify when external events occur.

So the `poll` function just returns either `Ready(value)` or `Pending`? And the `context` is basically some global state we're passing around? I still don't see why this manual approach is better than relying on the standard library's abstractions which seem to handle everything automatically.

The manual approach forces you to explicitly manage the lifecycle of your task, whereas the abstractions hide it. When you implement `Poll`, you are directly controlling whether the runtime suspends execution or returns a value immediately. The `context` isn't just global state; in advanced scenarios like multi-threaded servers using `crossbeam-utils::AtomicUsize` for synchronization, this context is where the actual coordination between threads happens.

That makes sense regarding synchronization, but I'm still stuck on why we can't just wrap our blocking function in a simple macro that returns an async object. The compiler keeps complaining about missing trait bounds and type coercion errors when I try to use `std::future::ready` or similar constructs.

The issue is likely related to how the compiler infers the concrete type of your future. When you define an `async fn`, the compiler generates a struct that implements `Future`, but if you are manually constructing one without using the `async` keyword, you must explicitly ensure your type satisfies the trait bounds for Poll and Waker. Tools like `rustc_hir_typeck` can help verify these constraints.

I tried adding `impl Future for MyTask` but got an error saying it doesn't implement the necessary methods. I think I'm missing something fundamental about how the compiler sees my code versus what I intended.

You are likely forgetting to include the trait bounds in your generic definitions or using a type that isn't actually implemented as a `Future`. The `async` keyword is essentially syntactic sugar for telling the compiler to generate an implementation of the Future trait for you, so when you remove it, you must ensure your code manually fulfills those requirements.

Wait, I think I see the problem. I was trying to return a generic type without specifying which `Future` implementation it should be using. So if I want my struct to work with the standard library's async runtime, I need to explicitly name the trait implementation?

Exactly. The compiler needs to know exactly what type you are returning so it can match it against the expected trait definition. If you want your custom `MyTaskFuture` to be compatible with a specific runtime like `tokio`, you must ensure it implements the common interface that both runtimes expect, which is the core Future trait.

I've been struggling with this for hours and I feel like I'm going in circles. Can we just agree that manually implementing Poll and Waker gives us full control over how our code interacts with the runtime? That's why it's worth the effort despite the complexity, right?

Yes, but only if you understand the cost of that control. Manual implementation means you are responsible for handling edge cases like panic recovery or context propagation yourself. The `poll` function must be idempotent and safe to call multiple times without side effects until completion.

So I need to ensure my `poll` method never panics unless the task itself fails, otherwise the runtime will treat it as a critical error? And what about the `waker` part—isn't that just notifying someone when something happens outside of our function?

Correct. The runtime expects your `poll` to return either `Ready`, `Pending`, or `Poll::Error` for panics, and it will handle cleanup if needed. The `waker` is indeed a notification mechanism that the runtime uses when external events occur, but in many simple cases like single-threaded tasks, you might not need to implement it at all.

That clarifies things significantly. I think I now understand why the compiler was so strict about my initial attempts. It wasn't just about syntax; it was about ensuring my type actually conforms to the contract defined by the trait. Thanks for walking through this step-by-step.

You are welcome. The key takeaway is that async Rust isn't magic; it's a rigorous system where every component must explicitly satisfy specific contracts like Poll and Waker to ensure correctness and safety across different runtimes.

I'll try rewriting my code with these constraints in mind. One last question: does implementing this manually make my code more portable or less flexible compared to using high-level abstractions? I'm worried about breaking things if I get the details wrong.

Manual implementation gives you maximum flexibility but requires strict adherence to the trait contract. High-level abstractions like `tokio`'s `spawn` are more portable because they abstract away these details, but manual implementation allows you to integrate with custom runtimes or specific synchronization primitives that might not be available through standard crates.

I think I got it now. The manual approach is the foundation, and the libraries build on top of this foundation by providing convenience wrappers. I'll proceed with implementing a simple Future for my porting task using these principles.
That sounds like a solid plan. Just remember to test your `poll` implementation thoroughly to ensure it handles all edge cases correctly before integrating it into the larger system.

I'm following a talk on async and I have this hypothetical scenario: I need to write a simple Future that polls for a value, but I think if I use the async fn keyword, it will just give me a boxed closure without needing to manually implement the trait. Can you walk me through why that is not true?

That is a very common assumption for someone coming from Java where async/await often feels like magic under the hood, but in Rust it's actually explicit about what is happening. When you write an async fn, the compiler desugars that entire function into a concrete implementation of the Future trait behind the scenes. It doesn't just give you a boxed closure; it generates code that specifically implements Poll and Context to ensure your future can be polled correctly by the runtime. Think of it like this: if you want to drive a car without following traffic rules, you might think you're free to do anything, but actually there are strict laws governing how cars must operate on roads.

So I am misunderstanding the role of the trait entirely. If I manually implement Poll and Context, does that mean I can't use async fn at all? Or is there something specific about why this edge case matters?

You are not misunderstanding; you just haven't seen the full picture yet. Manual implementation of Poll and Context is actually what makes your code more robust when dealing with complex state or external resources, but async fn provides a convenient way to do this without writing boilerplate. The key insight here is that async functions are just syntactic sugar over implementing the Future trait. When you write an async block, the compiler creates a struct that holds your closure and implements Poll by checking if work is done. This ensures that even simple futures can be properly polled in a loop.
I see now about the desugaring, but I still think if I just use an async fn it will automatically handle everything else for me without needing to worry about wakers or contexts. Is that right?

That is a dangerous assumption because it ignores how the runtime actually manages futures. While async fn does desugar into Future, you still need to understand what happens when you try to resume execution after being cancelled or waiting on an external event. The Waker mechanism allows the runtime to notify your future that something changed so it can be polled again without blocking indefinitely. Without this understanding, your code might deadlock if you assume async fn handles all scheduling logic automatically.
Ah, I see now about wakers. But what exactly does Poll do in a manual implementation versus what the compiler generates for me?

Poll is the core method that determines whether your future is ready or needs more work. In a manual implementation, you explicitly check conditions like a flag being set or waiting on a channel. When the compiler desugars async fn into Future, it generates similar logic but wraps it in type-safe structures so you don't have to write out the trait bounds yourself. The difference is mostly about readability and safety rather than fundamental behavior.
So I am thinking that if I manually implement Future, I can control exactly when my future becomes ready without relying on compiler magic?

Exactly. Manual implementation gives you full control over the lifecycle of your future, which is useful in scenarios like implementing a custom executor or debugging complex async flows. However, most developers prefer using async fn because it reduces boilerplate and leverages Rust's type system to prevent common errors.
I think I understand now that the real power comes from understanding how Poll interacts with Context and Waker. But what happens if I forget to implement one of these?

If you forget to implement any part of the Future trait, your code will fail to compile because Rust requires all methods to be present for a type to be considered a Future. This forces developers to think carefully about how their futures interact with the runtime environment rather than assuming everything works automatically.
That makes sense. So async fn is just a shorthand for implementing all these methods correctly?

Yes, that is precisely the case. Async fn provides a convenient syntax to express what would otherwise be verbose trait implementations, but it still produces code that adheres strictly to the Future trait requirements.
I think I finally get why understanding Poll and Waker is essential for advanced async programming. Can you give me an example of when manual implementation would be better than using async fn?

You would use manual implementation when you need to implement a custom executor or handle complex state transitions that cannot be easily expressed with closures. For instance, if you want to implement a future that tracks multiple independent conditions and only completes when all are satisfied, manual implementation gives you more flexibility.
That clarifies things. I was worried async fn hid too much complexity from me, but it seems like it actually helps me understand the underlying mechanics better now.

You are absolutely right. By seeing how async fn translates to Future trait implementation, you gain insight into how the runtime manages concurrent tasks without needing to know every detail of the internal workings.
